questions.questionText: The 'can't intubate, can't oxygenate' (CICO) situation requires immediate:
questions.optionB: Emergency front-of-neck access (cricothyrotomy/surgical airway — needle or surgical through cricothyroid membrane)
questions.explanation: Core concept: CICO = failed airway → emergency surgical access through cricothyroid membrane. Why correct: When all ventilation/intubation attempts fail → CICO → life-threatening → cricothyrotomy provides definitive emergency airway. Needle cricothyrotomy (14G cannula) in <12 years; surgical cricothyrotomy in adults. Why A wrong: Repeated attempts → trauma, edema, worsening situation. DAS guidelines limit to 3+1 intubation attempts. Why C wrong: May not be possible if muscle relaxants given and ventilation failing. Why D wrong: Blind techniques waste time in emergency. Clinical relevance: Cricothyroid membrane: between thyroid and cricoid cartilage — avascular, subcutaneous, easily palpable. Tracheostomy takes longer → not for emergency CICO. Exam trap: Cricothyrotomy (emergency) vs tracheostomy (elective/semi-emergency). In children <12: needle cricothyrotomy preferred (small caliber, risk of subglottic stenosis with surgical). Memory aid: 'CICO = Cut the Cricothyroid = Cannot do anything else.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Difficult Airway.

questions.questionText: Video laryngoscopy improves intubation success in difficult airways primarily by:
questions.optionB: Providing an indirect view of the glottis without requiring alignment of oral, pharyngeal, and laryngeal axes
questions.optionD: Eliminating the need for muscle relaxation
questions.explanation: Core concept: Video laryngoscopy doesn't require direct line-of-sight alignment. Why correct: Direct laryngoscopy requires alignment of 3 axes (oral, pharyngeal, laryngeal) — sniffing position. Video laryngoscopy: camera at blade tip provides indirect glottic view without aligning axes → higher success in difficult airways. Why A wrong: Tube stiffness unrelated to laryngoscope type (stylet can be used with either). Why C wrong: Tube size determined by patient anatomy, not scope type. Why D wrong: Muscle relaxation aids intubation regardless of laryngoscope type. Clinical relevance: Cormack-Lehane grading (I-IV) applies to glottic view with direct laryngoscopy. Grade III-IV = difficult → switch to video or alternative. Exam trap: Video laryngoscopy is now recommended as FIRST attempt in predicted difficult airways (NAP4, DAS guidelines). Memory aid: 'Video = View without aligning axes = Victory in difficult airway.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Airway Management.
questions.conceptTag: Video laryngoscopy indirect view difficult airway

questions.questionText: The Cormack-Lehane classification Grade III view during direct laryngoscopy shows:
questions.optionB: Only epiglottis visible (no glottic structures seen — predicted difficult intubation)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Cormack-Lehane grades predicted difficulty of intubation. Why correct: Grade I: full glottis visible (easy). Grade II: posterior commissure/arytenoids visible. Grade III: ONLY epiglottis visible (no glottis → difficult). Grade IV: no laryngeal structures visible (very difficult). Why A wrong: Full glottis = Grade I (easy). Why C wrong: Anterior commissure → Grade IIa/IIb classification. Why D wrong: Only soft palate = Mallampati Class IV (preoperative assessment, different classification). Clinical relevance: Grade III/IV → bougie-assisted intubation, video laryngoscopy, supraglottic airway device, or awake fiberoptic intubation. Exam trap: Mallampati (preoperative, mouth open) vs Cormack-Lehane (during laryngoscopy) — different assessments, both predict difficulty. Memory aid: 'CL = Cormack-Lehane: I=all, II=part, III=epiglottis only, IV=nothing.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Airway Assessment.
